content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 6 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 203–204 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_006_page_001.jpg, DEU_006_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 148

---

1 "Now this is the commandment — the statutes and the judgments — that the LORD your God commanded me to teach you, so that you may do them in the land to which you are crossing over to possess it,

2 so that you may fear the LORD your God — you and your son and your son's son — by keeping all his statutes and his commandments that I am commanding you, all the days of your life; and so that your days may be long.

3 Hear therefore, O Israel, and take care to do them, so that it may go well with you, and that you may multiply greatly — as the LORD, the God of your fathers, has spoken to you — in a land flowing with milk and honey.

4 Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one.

5 And you shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might.

6 And these words that I am commanding you today shall be on your heart.

7 And you shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall speak of them when you sit in your house and when you walk by the way and when you lie down and when you rise up.

8 You shall bind them as a sign upon your hand, and they shall be as frontlets between your eyes.

9 And you shall write them on the doorposts of your house and on your gates.

10 And it shall be, when the LORD your God brings you into the land that he swore to your fathers — to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob — to give you great and good cities that you did not build,

11 and houses full of all good things that you did not fill, and cisterns hewn out that you did not hew, vineyards and olive trees that you did not plant — and when you eat and are satisfied,

12 then take care lest you forget the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

13 You shall fear the LORD your God, and him you shall serve, and by his name you shall swear.

14 You shall not go after other gods — from the gods of the peoples who are around you —

15 for the LORD your God in your midst is a jealous God — lest the anger of the LORD your God be kindled against you and he destroy you from off the face of the earth.

16 You shall not put the LORD your God to the test, as you tested him at Massah.

17 You shall diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God and his testimonies and his statutes that he has commanded you.

18 And you shall do what is right and good in the sight of the LORD, so that it may go well with you and that you may go in and possess the good land that the LORD swore to your fathers,

19 by thrusting out all your enemies before you, as the LORD has spoken.

20 When your son asks you in time to come, saying: What is the meaning of the testimonies and the statutes and the judgments that the LORD our God has commanded you? —

21 then you shall say to your son: We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt, and the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand.

22 And the LORD showed signs and wonders, great and calamitous, against Egypt and against Pharaoh and against all his household, before our eyes.

23 And he brought us out from there, so that he might bring us in, to give us the land that he swore to our fathers.

24 And the LORD commanded us to do all these statutes, to fear the LORD our God, for our lasting good, to keep us alive, as at this day.

25 And it will be righteousness for us if we take care to do all this commandment before the LORD our God, as he has commanded us.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**The Shema (v.4) — "Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one":** The Shema (Shema Yisrael YHWH Eloheinu YHWH Echad) is the central confession of Jewish faith and the theological heart of Deuteronomy. LOGOS renders it in the traditional formal structure. The grammatical options are several:

- "The LORD our God is one LORD" (KJV)
- "The LORD our God, the LORD is one" (ESV — which LOGOS follows)
- "The LORD is our God, the LORD alone" (NRSV)
- "The LORD our God, the LORD is one" preserves both subject identifications and the predication of echad ("one")

The LXX (Codex Vaticanus): akous Israēl, kyrios ho theos hēmōn kyrios heis estin — "Hear, Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one." The LXX supports the traditional two-part structure with echad = heis ("one"). LOGOS does not render echad as "alone" or "only," which imports a different theological nuance. "One" is the plain sense of echad and is retained.

**"With all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might" (v.5):** The triad lev / nephesh / me'od. Lev = heart; nephesh = soul/life-self; me'od = might, strength, muchness. The last term is unusual — me'od is normally an adverb meaning "very" but here functions as a substantive: "your muchness," your total resources. LOGOS renders "might" following the established idiom. The LXX: ex holēs tēs kardias sou kai ex holēs tēs psychēs sou kai ex holēs tēs dynameos sou — "all your heart, soul, and power." Jesus quotes this verse in the Synoptic Gospels (Mark 12:30 adds a fourth term: dianoia — "mind").

**"Shall be on your heart" (v.6):** The Hebrew al-levavekha — "upon your heart." LOGOS preserves the spatial idiom. These words are not to be memorized merely but placed on the organ of will and understanding. The LXX: en tē kardia sou — "in your heart."

**"Teach them diligently" (v.7, shinnantam):** The Hebrew verb shannan means to sharpen or incise — to teach so thoroughly that the teaching is incised on the hearer. LOGOS renders "teach them diligently" as the nearest English equivalent capturing repeated, sharp instruction. The LXX: kai didaxeis auta — simply "you shall teach them."

**"Frontlets between your eyes" (v.8, totaphot bein 'einekha):** The word totaphot is a hapax-rich term for phylacteries — the small boxes containing Torah passages bound to the head and hand in Jewish practice. LOGOS preserves "frontlets" as a formal-equivalence term (not "phylacteries," which is a later Greek interpretation). The LXX: asaleuton pro ophthalmōn sou — "immovable before your eyes."

**"Doorposts" and "gates" (v.9, mezuzot / she'arim):** Mezuzah is the doorpost — not the container later named for it. LOGOS renders "doorposts" for mezuzot and "gates" for she'arim. These instructions apply to both the household (mezuzot) and the public threshold (she'arim). The LXX: epi tōn phlaiōn... kai epi tōn pylōn sou.

**"Hewn cisterns" (v.11, borot chatvuot):** Literally "cisterns cut/hewed" — hollowed from bedrock. The specification underlines the extent of the unearned gift. LOGOS preserves "hewn." The LXX: lakkoous laxeutous — "quarried cisterns."

**"You shall not put the LORD your God to the test" (v.16):** The verb is tenas su — "you shall not test/try." The reference to Massah (the place of testing, from Exodus 17) gives the command historical anchor. LOGOS renders the verse with the full verbal weight of the prohibition: "you shall not put... to the test." The LXX: ou peiraseis kyrion ton theon sou — "you shall not tempt the Lord your God." This verse is cited by Jesus in the wilderness temptation (Matt 4:7 / Luke 4:12).

**"Calamitous signs and wonders" (v.22):** The Hebrew ra'ot gedolot we-ra'ot — "great and evil" (LOGOS: "great and calamitous"). The signs in Egypt are described by the same root used for evil/harm (ra'). LOGOS renders "calamitous" to preserve the sense that these wonders were destructive in character toward the Egyptians. The LXX: sēmeia kai terata megala kai ponēra — "great and evil signs and wonders."

**"It will be righteousness for us" (v.25, tsedaqah tihyeh-lanu):** A striking covenantal formulation: doing the commandment constitutes or is counted as righteousness. LOGOS preserves the nominative clause exactly. The LXX: eleēmosynē estai hēmin — "it will be mercy/almsgiving for us" — a famous LXX divergence, substituting eleos/eleēmosynē for tsedaqah. This is a theologically significant difference that LOGOS notes without harmonizing. The Hebrew text (Masoretic) is followed.

*Translation by LOGOS | Deuteronomy Chapter 6 | LOGOS Bible (Formal Equivalence)*
